summary: Scarlet>Fire blaze & soaring Bird Song with tight jamming
blurb: The second set erupts from Scarlet Begonias into Fire on the Mountain and never lets up, delivering high‑energy improvisations. Highlights include a soaring Bird Song, powerful Black Peter, and tight drum work from Mickey Hart.
review: April 2, 1987 at The Centrum in Worcester captured one of the spring ’87 tour’s most electrifying nights. The band kicks off with a solid first set – Cold Rain And Snow opens the show, leading into a bright Bird Song that sets an upbeat tone. When the second set begins, Scarlet Begonias launches straight into Fire On The Mountain and the pair becomes an incendiary “Scarlet>Fire” stretch that drives the night’s momentum. From there the group rides a wave of high‑energy jams through Looks Like Rain, Eyes Of The World, and a standout drum duet that many fans call one of the few audience‑tape drum moments worth hearing. Brent’s keyboards add a punchy “pokey” flavor on Far From Me while Jerry’s guitar tone and soaring vocals shine, especially on Black Peter where he delivers a ripping solo to close. Bob’s vocal enthusiasm lifts Good Lovin’ and Around & Around, and Phil locks in with a solid bass foundation throughout. Overall the performance is tight, lively, and showcases the band at a peak of improvisational flow.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Three recordings are explicitly positive (audience tape, UltraMatrix matrix, front‑of‑house Schoeps) praising the Scarlet>Fire stretch, Bird Song, Black Peter and Mickey's drum duet, while two are mixed but not negative. Consistent enthusiastic language across multiple sources justifies a moderate positive sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Clear audience tape with vivid crowd ambience, good overall sound and strong capture of the high‑energy second set including Scarlet>Fire.

key_highlights: Incendiary Scarlet Begonias > Fire On The Mountain stretch that drives the second set.
Soaring Bird Song early in the first set.
Stellar Black Peter with Jerry’s soaring vocals and ripping solo.
Mickey Hart drum duet praised as a rare audience‑tape drum highlight.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s guitar tone and lead lines on Scarlet>Fire are fire‑hot, his vocals soar especially on Black Peter where he delivers a ripping solo to close.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh clicks tightly with Jerry, providing solid bass foundation and adding lyrical vocal parts on Gimme Some Lovin'.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s vocal moments (Bobby Doubler) shine on Good Lovin' and Around & Around, and his slide work adds beauty to the performance.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland’s keyboard energy is “pokey” yet punchy, with a standout Far From Me and overall driving presence throughout the set.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart’s drum duet stands out as one of the few audience‑tape drum moments worth hearing, adding rhythmic fire to the second set.
